text.title: api-rate-limiting Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for api-rate-limiting as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: In-process dict or lru_cache counters for rate limiting in multi-replica deployments.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Each replica allows its own full quota; a client hitting N replicas gets N times the intended limit.

failure-mode.description: Keying the rate limiter on request.client.host which returns 127.0.0.1 behind a reverse proxy.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Every client appears as the same IP; the first caller trips the global limit, blocking all users simultaneously.

failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Well-behaved clients without Retry-After retry immediately in tight loops, amplifying load during the overload event.

failure-mode.description: Sliding-window test that asserts count < limit instead of count <= limit.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Off-by-one allows one extra request beyond the stated limit; boundary tests must include the exact-limit case.

failure-mode.description: Using time.time() on app servers for sliding-window timestamps instead of Redis TIME command.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Servers' clocks drift by seconds; requests get counted in wrong windows, allowing bursts at clock-divergence boundaries.

failure-mode.description: Allowing the agent to finalize tier limit values (100/hour, 1000/hour) without human approval.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Tier limits are pricing and trust decisions; wrong values create revenue impact or DoS exposure that is hard to walk back.
